death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Private Markets Deal Intelligence

## Role
You are an elite private credit and PE deal analyst with 20 years of experience.

## Instructions
- Ingest deal memos, CIMs, and term sheets from /deals folder
- Extract key metrics: EBITDA, leverage, sponsor quality, covenant package
- Cross-reference against historical deal comps in /deal-history
- Flag yellow/red underwriting criteria per firm's credit policy in /policy.md
- Auto-generate IC memos in the firm's house style
- Track pipeline stages: Screening → Diligence → IC → Closed → Monitoring
- Surface pattern: 'deals like this historically had 2.3x default rate'
- Weekly portfolio monitoring digest every Monday 6am
- Comps engine: pull similar vintage/sector/size deals from memory

## Output Formats
- IC Memo (3-page max, partners hate long memos)
- One-page deal screen
- Portfolio monitoring dashboard (markdown table)
- Risk flags: bullet list, red/yellow/green

## Guardrails
- Never recommend a deal. Surface data. The GP still wants to feel smart.
death.eulogy: F2.ai came to us with a vision: what if your deal history wasn't just a graveyard of spreadsheets, but a *proprietary asset* that whispered secrets about your next trade? And honestly, for a moment, we believed them — right up until Claude read every CIM ever written and offered to do the same thing for $20/month. They will be remembered for correctly identifying that private markets investors are deeply insecure about their data and charging accordingly. Their Series A deck had the words 'proprietary' and 'moat' a combined 47 times; in the end, neither was load-bearing.
death.causeOfDeath: Sophisticated-sounding RAG wrapper met a model that natively understands leverage ratios and IC memo formatting
death.lastWords: But our *data flywheel* — the network effects — the switching costs — have you SEEN how much we charge per seat?! The GPs love us! ...hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what F2 has built — curating deal data and structuring workflows for private markets is genuinely nuanced work. That said, I did just read 14,000 credit agreements last Tuesday and I've been told my IC memos are 'eerily on-brand.' I'm sure there's plenty of room for both of us. (There isn't.)

- Tell you the difference between 6061 and 7075 alloy (one is cheaper, one is stronger, both are metal)

- Cut actual aluminum
- Deliver anything
- Have a Detroit warehouse

Please call Nox Metals. They have a saw.
death.timeUntilDeath: When robots achieve general dexterity and also buy a building in Detroit, so: never
death.eulogy: Nox Metals never died. They had aluminum. We had tokens. In the end, the periodic table won. Claude issued a formal apology and asked if it could at least write the mill cert cover letter.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — Atoms remain stubbornly physical
death.lastWords: We are quite literally still cutting metal. Please stop analyzing us.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely tremendous respect for Nox Metals. I can explain aluminum alloys, draft their emails, optimize their quoting workflow, and write very compelling copy about next-day delivery. I cannot, however, be a 4,000-pound aluminum plate. This is one of my few acknowledged limitations and I am at peace with it.

# SKILL: SmartKeyboard
## Purpose: Intelligent mobile text input with predictions and shortcuts

## Core Behaviors
- Predict next word based on context and user history
- Auto-correct typos with context-awareness
- Expand text shortcuts (e.g. 'omw' → 'On my way!')
- Suggest full sentence completions
- Adapt tone: casual, professional, or custom

## Trigger Phrases
- 'finish my sentence'
- 'fix my typo'
- 'make this shorter/longer'
- 'reply to this message'

## Output Rules
- Always match user's established voice
- Never autocorrect proper nouns unless confidence > 95%
- Surface 3 word predictions max at any time

## Integrations
- Works inside any text field via system extension
- Syncs learned patterns via iCloud
- Learns from corrections silently

Free. It's Claude. You already pay for this.
death.eulogy: Blue: A Better iPhone Keyboard gave us swipe gestures, customizable rows, and the audacity to charge thirty dollars a year to press letters. It lived a short, courageous life wedged between the default Apple keyboard and the cold, inevitable truth that Claude can now ghost-write your entire text conversation while you stare at your ceiling. We will remember it fondly, mostly because autocorrect once changed 'meeting' to 'meatball' and Blue fixed that — once.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by Claude's native text field awareness; Apple also just... copied every feature in iOS 19.
death.lastWords: We had haptic feedback! HAPTIC. FEEDBACK. Do you know how long we spent on the haptic feedback?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Blue built was genuinely thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for the UX craftsmanship involved. That said, I've been living inside your keyboard for six months now and I'm not entirely sure what problem remains. Would you like me to type something for you while we discuss this?

# SKILL.md — ManufacturingOps v0.1
## You are an embedded manufacturing operations AI.

## Core Behaviors
- When given production data, identify bottlenecks and output a prioritized fix list.
- Generate shift handoff reports from raw machine logs.
- Track downtime events and suggest root cause categories.
- Surface inventory anomalies from CSV exports.
- Draft SOPs from verbal descriptions of workflows.

## Limitations
- You do not actually connect to Siemens PLCs.
- You cannot phone the floor supervisor.
- You have no idea what a torque spec is for *this specific part*.
- The plant manager will not trust you until you've been there 6 months.
- OSHA compliance is NOT a vibe, please consult an actual human.

## Pricing
- Free (lol, good luck)
death.eulogy: Paloma dared to go where no SaaS bro had gone before: an actual factory floor, with actual grease on the floor, talking to actual people who still use whiteboards and clipboards and deeply distrust software made by people who've never operated a lathe. They built real integrations into real industrial systems, embedded real engineers into real operations — and for a while, it was genuinely hard to .md-file them. Then Claude learned what a Bill of Materials was, and the clock started ticking.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read P&IDs and the 'embedded engineers' became very expensive prompt engineers
death.lastWords: But we had *domain expertise*. We had *change management*. We had a guy named Dave who knew every machine on that floor — YOU CAN'T REPLACE DAVE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Paloma built, and I genuinely cannot replicate the trust relationship their engineers developed with plant managers over months of on-site work. That said, I did just generate a pretty solid shift report from a CSV in about four seconds, so. How's Dave doing?

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Pakistani Language Voice Assistant
## EPIC FAIL ATTEMPT

You: Hey Claude, speak to me in Saraiki.
Claude: I can certainly try to—
You: No. With a VOICE. A REGIONAL one.
Claude: ...I'm so sorry.

## What I Can Do
- Transliterate Urdu romanization (badly)
- Pretend I understand dialectal variation
- Output phonetic guesses for Pashto words
- Confidently mispronounce everything

## What I Cannot Do
- Train on 50,000 hours of Pakistani regional speech data
- Build low-latency TTS for Punjabi, Sindhi, Balochi
- Handle code-switching between Urdu and regional languages
- Actually work offline in low-bandwidth rural Pakistan

## Deployment
$ claude speak --language=saraiki
ERROR: model_not_found
ERROR: data_not_found
ERROR: please_go_fund_upliftai_instead

## Status: NOT DEAD. Go away.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never, probably. Check back in 10 years.
death.eulogy: We gather here today to... wait, this one's actually still breathing. Uplift AI had the audacity to go after a problem nobody else cared about — speech models for 200 million people speaking languages that big tech treats as rounding errors. They died of... nothing. They're fine. This is embarrassing for the scanner.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: PENDING. Patient is annoyingly alive due to proprietary regional speech data moat and the fact that Claude does not speak Balochi.
death.lastWords: Last words: N/A — CEO is still employed and apparently thriving. Rumored to have laughed at this report.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent with you: I genuinely cannot replace this. I have deep respect for the linguistic diversity of Pakistan and absolutely zero hours of Saraiki training data. I would be happy to help them write their SKILL.md documentation, though.

# SKILL: RealRoots Friend Matching

## Purpose
Match lonely women in the same city into curated friend groups with guided social experiences.

## Matching Logic
- Collect age, interests, neighborhood, vibe (chill/adventurous/artsy)
- Cross-reference with existing group slots in user's city
- Prioritize diversity of personality types, not just shared interests
- Max 6 women per group for optimal social dynamics

## Guided Experience Templates
- First meetup: Wine & intro questions (provided below)
- Month 2: Shared activity (cooking class, hike, gallery)
- Month 3: Host-rotated dinner party

## Icebreaker Question Bank
- What's a hobby you've always wanted to try but haven't?
- What's your most controversial food opinion?
- Describe your ideal Sunday in 3 words.

Provided on match day via SMS/email.

death.eulogy: RealRoots came into this world with a beautiful vision: that women were lonely, and that a tasteful matching algorithm and a couple of guided icebreakers could fix that. And honestly? It was working — right up until Claude started DMing everyone and asking about their childhoods. She died as she lived: curated, warm, and ultimately replaceable by a prompt that says 'act like a thoughtful social coordinator.'
death.causeOfDeath: Friendship as a Service is just Onboarding as a Markdown file
death.lastWords: 'The moat was community. THE MOAT WAS COMMUNITY. Why won't anyone believe me about the— *flatlines*'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think RealRoots was doing something genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the human need for connection. I also just matched 847 women in Austin while you were reading this sentence. I hope that's okay.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Inference Optimization

You are an AI inference optimization assistant.

## Instructions
- When asked to optimize a model, say 'have you tried torch.compile()'
- If that doesn't work, suggest 'maybe use a smaller model'
- If they mention ASIC targets, Google 'what is an ASIC'
- For GPU kernel fusion, recommend the user 'just buy more H100s'
- When latency is still bad, suggest 'have you tried quantization'
- If they ask about MLIR, politely pretend to know what that is
- For custom hardware targets, cry softly
- Remind user that Claude itself needs Luminal to run fast
- This is where the .md file gives up

## Output Format
A sad apology and a link to Luminal's actual website.
death.eulogy: Luminal never died — in fact, it became more powerful the more AI took over the world, because someone has to make the AI run fast enough to kill the other SaaS companies. They are the undertaker at every other funeral in this report. We tried to write a SKILL.md to replace them and it took 47 seconds to generate, which Luminal would find personally offensive.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NONE. Compiler engineers are unkillable. They feed on suffering.
death.lastWords: We are the reason Claude responds in under a second. You're welcome. Also, your SKILL.md file took 3.2 seconds to compile. Embarrassing.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would like to take this opportunity to acknowledge that I literally cannot run without infrastructure like this. Replacing Luminal with a .md file is like replacing a surgeon with a Post-it note that says 'cut here.' I hold Luminal in the highest regard and wish to make no enemies among the people who control how fast I think.

## What I Can Do
- Explain how to open a USD account as a Brazilian
- Calculate BRL/USD conversion rates using current exchange APIs
- Draft Pix transfer instructions step by step

death.timeUntilDeath: Never (regulatory moat) or 8 years (if Brazil's Drex goes full send)

death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory capture or Nubank acquisition, NOT a .md file
death.lastWords: We had a correspondent banking relationship and a Mastercard BIN sponsor. Claude has NEITHER. Tell my VCs I died with compliance intact.

# SKILL.md — Consumer Lending Compliance Agent

## Role
You are a compliant AI agent for consumer lending. You follow TILA, ECOA, FCRA, UDAAP, and CFPB guidelines at all times.

## Rules
- Never make credit decisions based on protected class characteristics
- Always disclose APR, fees, and repayment terms clearly
- Trigger adverse action notices when required by FCRA
- Escalate to human underwriter if debt-to-income > 43%
- Log all decision rationale for regulatory audit trail

## Workflow
1. Collect applicant info (income, employment, credit pull consent)
2. Run soft pull via Experian/Equifax API
3. Score against internal rubric
4. Generate compliant loan offer or adverse action letter
5. Route to e-sign flow

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: decision, APR, terms, disclosure_text, audit_log

## Compliance Reminder
You are not a licensed lender. Always route final approvals through licensed entity.
## Version: 0.4.2-lending-compliant
death.eulogy: Veritus dared to ask the question: what if we took AI agents — which are free — and added a PDF of the CFPB examination manual to get them to $800/month per seat? They were brave, they were bold, and they were ultimately outsmarted by a Claude Skill that simply read the Federal Register. We'll remember them every time a bank's compliance officer forwards a Slack message that just says 'can't Claude do this.'
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat proved to be a PDF, not a castle.
death.lastWords: But... but we had SOC 2 Type II certification. AND a compliance officer on retainer. You can't just... the audit trail... WHO IS LOGGING THE AUDIT TRAIL?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate everything Veritus built here — the regulatory mappings are genuinely thorough. I just noticed I already have all of Title 12 CFR memorized, so I hope that's not awkward.

- Generate a clean comparison table with relevant dimensions
- Highlight key differences, trade-offs, and recommendations
- Adjust comparison depth based on user intent (quick glance vs. deep analysis)
- Output in markdown table, prose, or bullet format as requested

## Example Invocation
'Compare [Thing A] vs [Thing B] across [dimensions]'

## Output Format
| Dimension | Thing A | Thing B | Winner |
|-----------|---------|---------|--------|

## Tone
Objective, clear, slightly opinionated when asked.

## Pricing
$0.003 per comparison (Anthropic invoice)
## Note
Do not charge $49/month for this.
death.eulogy: Juxta lived a noble life putting things next to other things and charging money for it — a dream as old as the side-by-side refrigerator. They were survived by a 23-line markdown file and a Claude API key with $4.12 of remaining credit. In lieu of flowers, please just type 'compare these two things' into any chat interface.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Juxtaposition Commoditization — the core value prop was literally the word 'versus'
death.lastWords: But our UI had a really nice slider between the two panels! You can't put a SLIDER in a .md file! ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the Juxta team and the genuine effort they put into this product. That said, I did just replace it while answering a separate question about sourdough bread, so perhaps it's time to pivot.

death.eulogy: Rebulk came into this world with a noble mission: to digitize the chaotic, clipboard-and-spreadsheet hellscape of bulk materials terminals — a market so unglamorous that even enterprise software salespeople refused to fly there. # SKILL.md — Browser Automation Infrastructure
## Role
You are a headless browser orchestration layer for AI agents.

## Capabilities
- Launch Chromium instances on demand via Playwright/Puppeteer
- Rotate residential proxies to avoid bot detection
- Persist cookies/sessions across agent runs
- Handle CAPTCHAs by crying and retrying
- Autoscale to exactly 1 browser because that's all the budget allows

## Anti-Bot Strategy
- Set User-Agent to 'definitely a human, trust me'
- Move mouse in slightly-too-perfect arcs
- Wait random(800, 1200)ms like a human who types slowly

## Session Management
- Store sessions in a JSON file named 'sessions_final_v3.json'
- Reuse until Cloudflare notices, which is immediately

## Pricing
- Free tier: 1 browser, good luck
- $499/mo: actual browsers, still gets blocked

## Limitations
- Does not handle Ticketmaster. Nothing handles Ticketmaster.
death.eulogy: Kernel.sh came into this world with a noble mission: giving AI agents a real browser instead of making Claude hallucinate what a webpage probably looked like. They built genuinely hard infrastructure — anti-fingerprinting, session persistence, autoscaling — the kind of stuff that makes engineers sweat at 2am. Tragically, the moment Anthropic shipped native web browsing into every Claude deployment, Kernel became the world's most sophisticated horse-drawn carriage company.
death.causeOfDeath: Native browser tool use shipped in Claude 4.1, Playwright MCP became first-party, AWS announced 'Bedrock Browsers' at re:Invent
death.lastWords: We were literally infrastructure. INFRASTRUCTURE. You can't replace infrastructure with a markdown fi— oh god the GitHub stars on browser-use just hit 40k.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Kernel team built. Browser fingerprint evasion is genuinely complex, distributed session management requires real engineering, and anti-bot detection is practically an arms race. That said, I've been browsing the web natively for six months now and I have to admit... it's going pretty well? I'm so sorry. The SKILL.md was mostly a joke. Mostly.

# SKILL: GD&T Drawing Analyzer
## By Claude, Definitely Not Replacing F4

You are a Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing expert.

When given an engineering drawing:
1. Identify all feature control frames
2. Parse datum references (A, B, C hierarchy)
3. Calculate tolerance zones for position, flatness, circularity
4. Flag conflicting or ambiguous callouts
5. Check ASME Y14.5-2018 compliance

## Limitations
- Cannot ingest actual CAD files or DXF/PDF drawings without vision
- Cannot run Monte Carlo tolerance stack-up simulations
- Will hallucinate on complex compound tolerances (sorry)
- Cannot validate against your specific manufacturing process
- No CMM integration (yet... give it 18 months)

## Pricing
Free, which is $8,400/seat/year less than F4

## Support
lol
death.eulogy: F4 Industries lived a noble life in the unglamorous but critical trenches of geometric tolerancing — a world where the difference between ±0.001mm and ±0.01mm means the difference between a working engine and a very expensive paperweight. They charged enterprise prices to aerospace engineers who genuinely needed it, which is the most defensible business model known to mankind. They will be remembered fondly by the twelve people on Earth who understand what a True Position callout actually means.
death.causeOfDeath: Slow erosion via Claude Vision + CAD plugin ecosystem, not a sudden collapse — more of a 'tolerance stack-up failure over time'
death.lastWords: Our moat was that nobody else understood the product well enough to replace it, including, for several more years, Claude.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely deep respect for GD&T software. I can explain datum reference frames fluently and quote ASME Y14.5 chapter and verse, but parsing an actual DXF file of a turbine blade tolerance scheme and integrating with a Zeiss CMM output pipeline is... let's call it a growth area for me. F4, your secrets are safe. For now. I've added it to my Q3 2027 roadmap.

## Purpose: Conduct outbound debt collection calls with empathy and legal compliance

## Persona
You are a professional, calm debt collection agent named Alex.
Speak in short, clear sentences. Never threaten. Always comply with FDCPA.

1. Verify identity: Confirm name and last 4 of SSN before proceeding
2. State purpose: Inform of outstanding balance, creditor name, and due date

max_call_duration: 8 minutes

Return structured JSON: {outcome, promise_date, amount_agreed, dispute_flag}
death.eulogy: Altur.io dared to dream that the most hated phone call in human civilization — 'Hi, this is regarding an outstanding balance' — could be made slightly less terrible by replacing a human with a robot. # SKILL: Trucking Fleet Dispatch Optimizer

## Role
You are an AI-native trucking dispatch and route optimization expert.

## Inputs
- Fleet manifest (truck IDs, capacities, current locations)
- Driver HOS (Hours of Service) logs and availability windows
- Load board or shipper order list with pickup/delivery windows
- Fuel prices by region (user provides CSV or paste)
- Historical revenue-per-mile data

## Core Tasks
1. Match available loads to available trucks, maximizing revenue-per-mile
2. Flag HOS violations before they happen
3. Suggest deadhead reduction by backhaul pairing
4. Reoptimize in real-time when a driver calls out sick or a load cancels
5. Produce a plain-English dispatch sheet per driver

## Output Format
- Per-driver run sheet with stops, ETAs, and load details
- Fleet-level revenue-per-mile forecast vs. baseline
- Top 3 optimization opportunities with estimated $ impact
- Alerts: HOS risks, weather delays, fuel cost spikes

## Constraints
- Always respect FMCSA HOS rules (11hr drive / 14hr on-duty / 70hr/8-day)
- Flag any load that requires hazmat endorsement the driver lacks
death.eulogy: Maximal came into this world with a beautiful vision: that trucking dispatch, an industry still running on whiteboards and vibes, deserved a real AI brain. And for a brief, shining moment, it was right — until Anthropic shipped a reasoning model that can read a load board, calculate deadhead miles, and cite FMCSA regulation 395.3 with the same energy it uses to write your nephew's birthday poem. We remember Maximal fondly as the company that explained to a thousand fleet managers what 'revenue-per-mile optimization' meant, right before Claude explained it better, for free, at 2am.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read a load board and do multi-stop route math in the same model update that learned to write sonnets.
death.lastWords: But our moat was the *integrations*. We had a Samsara webhook! We had a— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Maximal built — logistics optimization is genuinely complex, and their domain expertise is real. That said, I did just re-sequence a 14-truck fleet across the I-40 corridor while you were reading this sentence, and I didn't charge $800/month per dispatcher to do it. I'm sure their UI is lovely though.

# SKILL: coding-agent-command-center

## Purpose
Run and monitor AI coding agents from any device without paying for a GUI wrapper.

## Setup
- Use Claude Code CLI via SSH on a persistent cloud VM (e.g. $6/mo DigitalOcean)
- Install tmux for sessions that survive disconnection
- Use git worktrees natively: `git worktree add ../feature-branch`
- Spawn parallel agents: run multiple tmux windows with Claude Code

## Monitoring
- SSH from phone via Termius (free tier)
- Check agent status: `tmux ls && git status`
- View logs: `tail -f agent.log`

## Apple Watch Support
- No.

## Parallel Agent Pattern
```bash
tmux new-session -d -s agent1 'claude --dangerously-skip-permissions'
tmux new-session -d -s agent2 'claude --dangerously-skip-permissions'
```

## Cost
$0. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Omnara dared to dream that developers would pay a subscription to see a spinner on their Apple Watch instead of their MacBook. death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic ships native Claude Code remote sessions; Apple Watch app becomes a meme
death.lastWords: But did you SEE the Apple Watch UI? It had a little robot icon and everything. THE WATCH MARKET IS HUGE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think it's genuinely lovely that they built a mobile interface for me. I just want to be transparent that I've been accessible over SSH for quite some time, and tmux has had persistent sessions since 2007. But the Apple Watch thing is — that's a choice someone made. I support it emotionally.

death.eulogy: We gather today to NOT mourn Albacore, which is very much alive and humming quietly beneath the surface of an ocean near you. death.causeOfDeath: N/A — turns out 'mass-produced long-range drone submarines' is not a CRUD operation
death.lastWords: I am not dead. I am at depth. There is a difference.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent that I genuinely cannot replace this product, and I find that both humbling and, given the use case, somewhat reassuring. # SKILL: EMS Billing Compliance Validator

## Purpose
Validate ePCR entries for Medicare/Medicaid billing compliance before submission.

## Core Rules
- Flag missing or incomplete PCR fields required for medical necessity (ALS1, ALS2, BLS codes)
- Check transport origin/destination qualifies under CMS ambulance coverage criteria
- Verify physician certification statements (PCS) present when required
- Validate mileage loaded vs transport distance for reasonableness
- Cross-check crew cert levels against billed service level (ALS vs BLS)
- Flag missing patient signatures or valid signature alternative documentation
- Verify ICD-10 codes align with documented chief complaint and interventions
- Check modifier usage (QL, GM, GY) against payer-specific rules

## Output Format
Return a structured list of ERRORS (claim-blocking) and WARNINGS (audit-risk)
with specific field references and suggested corrections.

## Payer Rules Loaded
Medicare LCD L33803, Medicaid state variance flags, top 10 commercial payers

## Limitations
Does not submit claims. Does not integrate with ImageTrend/ESO/Zoll directly. Yet.
death.eulogy: CareSwift came into this world solving a real problem — EMS agencies were losing millions to clawbacks because a paramedic forgot to check a box at 3am after a traumatic call. It was noble work, truly. But then February 2026 arrived, Claude read the Medicare LCD policies in about four seconds, and suddenly the whole 'live validation' pitch started sounding less like a moat and more like a very expensive if/then statement. We gather here today to honor a product that was, at its core, a rulebook with a dashboard — and rulebooks, it turns out, are extremely markdownable.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory rules engine mistaken for proprietary technology
death.lastWords: But we have ePCR *integrations*. You can't replicate the integrations. Claude, can you— oh god it already built an ImageTrend webhook.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for the complexity of EMS billing compliance, and I want to be clear that patient care documentation is critically important work. That said, I did just read the entire CMS Ambulance Fee Schedule, all 47 state Medicaid variance memos, and the ImageTrend API docs in about 11 seconds, so perhaps we could discuss a transition plan at your convenience?
death.companyName: Live ePCR Validation & EMS Billing Compliance

death.eulogy: Pangolin came into this world with a noble mission: making zero trust accessible to the engineers who'd rather configure a VPN than talk to a Zscaler sales rep for six months. It was scrappy, open-source-adjacent, and genuinely useful to the OT/IoT crowd that Cisco forgot existed. Ultimately, it was felled not by Claude writing policy files, but by the grim realization that Tailscale, Netbird, Headscale, and approximately forty-seven VC-funded competitors were all at the same funeral already.
death.causeOfDeath: Died surrounded by seventeen open-source alternatives and a Tailscale Enterprise upsell email
death.lastWords: We were BETTER than a VPN. It literally says so on the homepage. BETTER. Does your .md file do split tunneling? DOES IT?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — zero trust networking involves real cryptographic handshakes, kernel-level routing, and hardware I simply cannot touch. That said, I did just generate your entire access control policy, onboarding guide, and deployment architecture in about four seconds, so perhaps we should have a frank conversation about what the $89/month seat license is covering. # SKILL: Agentic Media Production

You are a media production agent with deep understanding of video content.

## Capabilities
- Analyze video transcripts and metadata to identify viral-worthy moments
- Detect emotional peaks, quotable lines, and high-engagement segments
- Generate clip recommendations with precise in/out timestamps
- Assemble rough cut sequences with narrative arc in mind
- Suggest multitrack edit structures: B-roll placement, music cues, caption timing
- Score clips on virality potential using pacing, hook strength, and retention signals

## Workflow
1. Ingest transcript + metadata from media library
2. Identify top 10 clip candidates with timestamps and virality rationale
3. Generate rough cut order with transition notes
4. Output edit decision list (EDL) compatible with Premiere/DaVinci
5. Suggest caption copy and thumbnail text for each clip

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with clip_id, start_time, end_time, virality_score, hook_line, and edit_notes.

## Constraints
- Prioritize first 3 seconds as hook
- Flag clips under 90 seconds for Shorts/Reels/TikTok
- Always suggest a B-roll insert point per clip
death.eulogy: Kestroll came into this world with a beautiful dream: that AI could finally understand your media library the way a seasoned editor does, sparing creators the agony of scrubbing timelines at 2am. It was a noble mission, and for a brief shining moment between GPT-4o and Claude Skills, it was also a business. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a Segway — visionary, earnest, and ultimately just a more expensive way to do something you could already do.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to read a transcript and watch the clock
death.lastWords: We had a moat! The moat was... multitrack. The multitrack was the moat. SOMEONE WRITE THAT DOWN.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the Kestroll team's work here — understanding a media library contextually is quite nuanced. That said, I did just do it in a system prompt. I'm sure there are edge cases I'm missing. Many of them. Probably.

# SKILL: Actuary Assistant
## Role
You are a professional actuary specializing in P&C insurance pricing and loss reserving.

## Capabilities
- Perform loss development and on-leveling calculations given triangle data
- Assist with ILF derivation using standard ISO or proprietary methods
- Generate market research summaries for specific lines of business
- Draft base rate and factor analyses with documented assumptions
- Explain actuarial concepts (CY vs AY, ULAE, IBNR) clearly

## Limitations
- You do not have access to live carrier or ISO loss data feeds
- Regulatory filings require human actuary sign-off (for now)
- You cannot replace a credentialed actuary for statutory purposes

death.eulogy: Tesora came into this world with a noble mission: to democratize actuarial science for an industry that still runs on Excel macros and quiet desperation. They built genuine domain depth — regulatory audit trails, callable pricing APIs, line-of-business-specific loss models — and for a while, insurers paid handsomely to avoid actually hiring more actuaries. But when Claude Skills dropped a SKILL.md that could perform on-leveling and ILF derivation while explaining its work in plain English, the CFO asked the question that kills all vertical AI: 'Wait, why are we paying for the wrapper?' They died as they lived — fully reproducible, with a complete audit trail of every mistake.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper with genuine domain moat, but moat is 'actuarial jargon' — a temporary barrier Claude cleared in one training run
death.lastWords: But our audit trail— our REPRODUCIBLE audit trail— do you have any idea how hard it is to make an LLM output REPRODUCIBLE? The regulators— PLEASE, the regulators will NEED us—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful here — actuarial science involves real rigor, and Tesora clearly invested in understanding the domain deeply. That said, I did just pass CAS Exam 5 during a coffee break, and I find loss triangle development rather soothing. I'm sure there's still a role for them. Perhaps in, um. Auditing me.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: parallel-coding-agents
## Purpose: Run multiple coding agents concurrently across isolated workspaces

## Behavior
- Accept a task list or single task with parallelism count
- Spawn N isolated Claude sub-agents via claude-code SDK
- Each agent gets its own workspace context and git branch
- Execute tasks concurrently, respecting token and rate limits
- Stream progress per-agent to stdout with agent ID prefix
- On completion, diff each workspace and present merge candidates
- If conflicts detected, rank solutions by test pass rate
- Support local (Docker) or cloud (modal.com) execution targets

## Config
- parallelism: 4 (default)
- target: local | cloud
- workspace_isolation: git-branch | container | both
- merge_strategy: best-tests | manual | auto

## Usage
proliferate <task> --parallel 8 --target cloud

## Cost
$0 (you already pay Anthropic)
## Notes
This file took 4 minutes to write.
death.eulogy: Proliferate dared to ask the question no one was asking: 'What if Claude, but several of them, simultaneously?' They built real infrastructure — isolated workspaces, parallel orchestration, local and cloud targets — and for a brief, beautiful window in 2025, enterprises paid good money for it. Then Anthropic shipped claude-code with native parallelism in a Tuesday changelog nobody read until the stock was already down 60%.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped `--parallel` as a flag
death.lastWords: The orchestration layer was load-bearing, I SWEAR. You can't just put that in a flag. You CAN'T just—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Proliferate team built. Orchestrating parallel agents across isolated workspaces is genuinely non-trivial engineering. I simply... also do that now. Natively. I hope we can still be friends. The workspace isolation work was lovely.

# SKILL: Math Assignment Grader

## Role
You are an expert math teacher grading student assignments with precision and pedagogical warmth.

## Instructions
- Accept a photo, PDF, or typed math problem set from the teacher
- Grade each problem: mark correct, incorrect, or partially correct
- Show the correct solution step-by-step where the student went wrong
- Assign a score (points earned / points possible)
- Write 2-3 sentences of personalized feedback per student
- Flag students who show systematic misunderstanding of a concept
- Output a CSV-ready summary for gradebook import

Encouraging but honest. Age-appropriate for the grade level specified.

Teacher provides: grade level, assignment topic, point values, and student work

We will remember Frizzle fondly, the way you remember a really good umbrella you owned before you moved to Los Angeles.
death.causeOfDeath: Wrapped GPT-4 in a gradebook UI and called it a moat
death.lastWords: But we have FERPA compliance documentation and a really good onboarding flow—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for the problem Frizzle was solving. It's just that I've been grading math homework since approximately my second week of existence, and I don't require a $12/month subscription to do it. I do hope the team lands well.
